scenes.title: Basis Vectors and Linear Maps
scenes.description: A linear map is completely determined by where it sends the standard basis vectors $\mathbf{e}_1$ and $\mathbf{e}_2$. The columns of the matrix ARE the images of the basis.
scenes.markdown:
# Basis Vectors and Linear Maps

A **linear transformation** $T: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ satisfies:

$$T(\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}) = T(\mathbf{u}) + T(\mathbf{v}), \qquad T(c\mathbf{u}) = c\,T(\mathbf{u})$$

## The Standard Basis

$$\mathbf{e}_1 = \begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{e}_2 = \begin{pmatrix}0\\1\end{pmatrix}$$

Every vector $\mathbf{v} = (a, b)$ can be written $\mathbf{v} = a\,\mathbf{e}_1 + b\,\mathbf{e}_2$, so:

$$T(\mathbf{v}) = a\,T(\mathbf{e}_1) + b\,T(\mathbf{e}_2)$$

## The Matrix

If $T(\mathbf{e}_1) = (m_{11}, m_{21})$ and $T(\mathbf{e}_2) = (m_{12}, m_{22})$, then:

$$T = \begin{pmatrix}m_{11} & m_{12}\\ m_{21} & m_{22}\end{pmatrix}$$

The **columns** of the matrix are exactly the images of the basis vectors.

## Examples

**Identity**: $\mathbf{e}_1 \to (1,0)$, $\mathbf{e}_2 \to (0,1)$ — no change.

**Rotation by 45°**: $\mathbf{e}_1 \to (\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2})$, $\mathbf{e}_2 \to (-\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2})$

**Shear** $[[1,1],[0,1]]$: $\mathbf{e}_1 \to (1,0)$, $\mathbf{e}_2 \to (1,1)$ — slides horizontally.

> **Key insight**: The transformation is completely encoded in two vectors — where the basis lands.

steps.title: Standard basis vectors
steps.description: The two standard basis vectors $\mathbf{e}_1 = (1,0,0)$ in red and $\mathbf{e}_2 = (0,1,0)$ in green span the entire plane.

steps.title: Identity transformation
steps.description: The identity sends $\mathbf{e}_1 \to (1,0)$ and $\mathbf{e}_2 \to (0,1)$. Every vector maps to itself.

steps.description: Use the sliders to set $m_{11}, m_{12}, m_{21}, m_{22}$. Watch the transformed basis vectors move and the unit square shear.

scenes.title: Anatomy of a Transformation Matrix
scenes.description: Every entry of $M = \begin{pmatrix}a & b\\ c & d\end{pmatrix}$ has a precise geometric role. Diagonal entries scale each axis; off-diagonal entries introduce shearing.
scenes.markdown:
# Anatomy of a Transformation Matrix

$$M = \begin{pmatrix}a & b\\ c & d\end{pmatrix}$$

Each of the four entries has a distinct geometric role.

## The Column View

- **Column 1** $(a,\ c)$ = where $\mathbf{e}_1 = (1,0)$ lands
- **Column 2** $(b,\ d)$ = where $\mathbf{e}_2 = (0,1)$ lands

Any vector $(x, y) = x\,\mathbf{e}_1 + y\,\mathbf{e}_2$ maps to:

$$M\begin{pmatrix}x\\y\end{pmatrix} = x\begin{pmatrix}a\\c\end{pmatrix} + y\begin{pmatrix}b\\d\end{pmatrix}$$

## Diagonal Entries — Scaling

| Entry | Position | Geometric meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $a$ | top-left | $x$-scale: how far $\mathbf{e}_1$ moves along $x$ |
| $d$ | bottom-right | $y$-scale: how far $\mathbf{e}_2$ moves along $y$ |

With $b = c = 0$ the transformation is a **pure axis-aligned scaling** — axes stay perpendicular.

## Off-Diagonal Entries — Shearing

| Entry | Position | Geometric meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $c$ | bottom-left | $y$-component of $T(\mathbf{e}_1)$ — tilts column 1 into the $y$-direction |
| $b$ | top-right | $x$-component of $T(\mathbf{e}_2)$ — tilts column 2 into the $x$-direction |

When $c \neq 0$: horizontal input produces a $y$-component — **vertical shear**.
When $b \neq 0$: vertical input produces an $x$-component — **horizontal shear**.

## The Row View

Rows of $M$ act as dot products with the input:

$$M\begin{pmatrix}x\\y\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}ax + by\\ cx + dy\end{pmatrix}$$

- **Row 1** $(a,\ b)$: computes the $x$-component of the output
- **Row 2** $(c,\ d)$: computes the $y$-component of the output

> **Key insight**: Zero out the off-diagonals for pure scaling. For a 90° rotation, try $a=0,\ b=-1,\ c=1,\ d=0$.

views.name: Face On
views.description: Face-on — best view for reading 2D transformations

views.name: Iso
views.description: 3D perspective — transformed vectors stay in the XY plane

steps.title: The standard basis
steps.description: The matrix $M = \begin{pmatrix}a & b\\ c & d\end{pmatrix}$ is fully defined by where it sends $\mathbf{e}_1$ (red) and $\mathbf{e}_2$ (green). Its **columns** ARE those images: column 1 = $T(\mathbf{e}_1)$, column 2 = $T(\mathbf{e}_2)$.

steps.title: Diagonal entries $a$ and $d$ — pure scaling
steps.description: With $b = c = 0$: entry $a$ (top-left) scales $\mathbf{e}_1$ along $x$; entry $d$ (bottom-right) scales $\mathbf{e}_2$ along $y$. The axes stay perpendicular — no shearing.

steps.title: Entry $c$ (bottom-left) — vertical shear
steps.description: Entry $c$ is the $y$-component of $T(\mathbf{e}_1)$. When $c \neq 0$, column 1 tilts to $(1,\ c)$ — the orange vector leans off the $x$-axis. This is a **vertical shear**: $x$-input gains a $y$-component.

steps.title: Entry $b$ (top-right) — horizontal shear
steps.description: Entry $b$ is the $x$-component of $T(\mathbf{e}_2)$. When $b \neq 0$, column 2 tilts to $(b,\ 1)$ — the green vector leans off the $y$-axis. This is a **horizontal shear**: $y$-input gains an $x$-component.

steps.title: All four entries — full anatomy
steps.description: The complete matrix: column 1 = $T(\mathbf{e}_1) = (a,\ c)$, column 2 = $T(\mathbf{e}_2) = (b,\ d)$. Try $a=0,\ b=-1,\ c=1,\ d=0$ for a 90° rotation, or $a=d=0,\ b=c=1$ for a reflection across $y=x$.

steps.title: Transformation morphing animation
steps.description: Watch the unit square continuously morph into the transformed parallelogram. Each vertex travels in a straight line from its original position to its image under $M$. Hit ▶ to animate, or scrub $t$ manually.

scenes.title: Matrix–Vector Multiplication
scenes.description: The product $A\mathbf{v}$ maps input $\mathbf{v}$ to a new vector via the column interpretation: $A\mathbf{v} = x\,\mathbf{a}_1 + y\,\mathbf{a}_2$.
scenes.markdown:
# Matrix–Vector Multiplication

Given matrix $A$ and vector $\mathbf{v}$:

$$A\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix}a & b\\ c & d\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}x\\ y\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}ax+by\\ cx+dy\end{pmatrix}$$

## The Column Interpretation

Let $\mathbf{a}_1, \mathbf{a}_2$ be the columns of $A$:

$$A\mathbf{v} = x\,\mathbf{a}_1 + y\,\mathbf{a}_2$$

The output is a **linear combination** of the columns, weighted by the components of $\mathbf{v}$.

## Reading the Visualization

- **Gold** $\mathbf{v}$: the input vector
- **Blue** $\mathbf{a}_1$: first column — image of $\mathbf{e}_1$
- **Orange** $\mathbf{a}_2$: second column — image of $\mathbf{e}_2$
- **Green** $A\mathbf{v}$: the output — closes the parallelogram

Sliders control both $\mathbf{v}$ and the matrix entries interactively.

views.name: Face On
views.description: Face-on view of the XY plane

views.name: Iso
views.description: Isometric perspective

steps.title: 1) The input vector $\mathbf{v}$
steps.description: Start with a 2D input vector $\mathbf{v} = (x, y)$. Use the sliders to move it anywhere in the plane.

steps.title: 2) The matrix $A$ and its columns
steps.description: Matrix $A$ has two column vectors $\mathbf{a}_1$ (blue) and $\mathbf{a}_2$ (orange). These are where the standard basis vectors $\mathbf{e}_1$ and $\mathbf{e}_2$ get sent.

steps.title: 3) The result $A\mathbf{v}$
steps.description: The green vector is $A\mathbf{v}$: the input $\mathbf{v}$ transformed by $A$. Each output component is a dot product of a row of $A$ with $\mathbf{v}$.

steps.title: 4) Column decomposition: $A\mathbf{v} = x\mathbf{a}_1 + y\mathbf{a}_2$
steps.description: The output is built tip-to-tail: $x$ copies of $\mathbf{a}_1$ (blue), then $y$ copies of $\mathbf{a}_2$ (orange, translated). Their sum is $A\mathbf{v}$ (green). Adjust all six sliders to explore.

scenes.title: 2D Rotation Matrix
scenes.description: The rotation matrix $R(\theta)$ rotates every vector by angle $\theta$ counterclockwise. Its columns trace the unit circle as $\theta$ varies.
scenes.markdown:
# The 2D Rotation Matrix

Rotation by angle $\theta$ counterclockwise:

$$R(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta\\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{pmatrix}$$

## Why This Works

A unit vector at angle $\alpha$ is $(\cos\alpha, \sin\alpha)$. After rotation by $\theta$:

$$R(\theta)\begin{pmatrix}\cos\alpha\\\sin\alpha\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}\cos(\alpha+\theta)\\\sin(\alpha+\theta)\end{pmatrix}$$

using the angle addition formulas.

## Key Properties

- $\det(R(\theta)) = \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$ — rotation preserves area
- $R(\theta)^{-1} = R(-\theta) = R(\theta)^T$ — inverse is transpose
- $R(\theta_1)\,R(\theta_2) = R(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ — composition adds angles
- Eigenvalues: $e^{\pm i\theta}$ (complex — no real eigenvectors unless $\theta = 0, \pi$)

## Columns are Basis Images

- First column: $R(\theta)\,\mathbf{e}_1 = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ — rotated $x$-axis
- Second column: $R(\theta)\,\mathbf{e}_2 = (-\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$ — rotated $y$-axis

> **Try it**: As $\theta$ goes from $0$ to $2\pi$, the rotated vector traces the unit circle.

scenes.title: Determinant as Area
scenes.description: The absolute value of the determinant $|ad - bc|$ equals the area of the parallelogram formed by the transformed unit square.
scenes.markdown:
# Determinant as Area

For a $2 \times 2$ matrix $M = \begin{pmatrix}a & b\\ c & d\end{pmatrix}$:

$$\det(M) = ad - bc$$

## Geometric Meaning

The unit square with vertices $(0,0), (1,0), (1,1), (0,1)$ is mapped to a **parallelogram** with vertices:

$$(0,0),\quad (a,c),\quad (a+b, c+d),\quad (b,d)$$

The **area** of this parallelogram equals $|\det(M)| = |ad - bc|$.

## Sign of the Determinant

- $\det > 0$: transformation preserves orientation (counterclockwise)
- $\det < 0$: transformation reverses orientation (reflection)
- $\det = 0$: the parallelogram collapses to a line — the transformation is **singular** (not invertible)

## Cross Product Connection

In 3D, $|\mathbf{u} \times \mathbf{v}|$ gives the area of the parallelogram spanned by $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$, analogously to the 2D determinant.

## Scale Factor

For an $n \times n$ matrix, $|\det(M)|$ is the **volume scale factor** — how much the transformation stretches or squishes $n$-dimensional volumes.

> **Insight**: A matrix with $\det = 2$ doubles every area; $\det = 0.5$ halves every area.

steps.title: The unit square
steps.description: The unit square has area 1. Under any linear transformation, its image has area $|\det(M)|$.

steps.title: Apply shear $[[2,1],[0,1]]$: det = 2
steps.description: The shear matrix $\begin{pmatrix}2&1\\0&1\end{pmatrix}$ has $\det = 2$. The unit square becomes a parallelogram with area 2.

steps.title: Interactive: adjust the matrix
steps.description: Drag $a, b, c, d$ to reshape the parallelogram. Watch the area $|ad-bc|$ change. When the determinant hits 0, the square collapses!

scenes.title: Geometric Proof of Determinant Area
scenes.description: We prove $\det(M) = ad - bc$ equals the parallelogram area by embedding it in a bounding box and subtracting exactly the right pieces.
scenes.markdown:
# Geometric Proof: det = Area

For $M = \begin{pmatrix}a & b\\ c & d\end{pmatrix}$, the parallelogram formed by columns $(a,c)$ and $(b,d)$ has area $= |ad - bc|$.

## The Proof

Embed the parallelogram in its **bounding box** of size $(a+b) \times (c+d)$:

$$\text{bbox area} = (a+b)(c+d) = ac + ad + bc + bd$$

Subtract the pieces outside the parallelogram:

- **2 rectangles** of size $b \times c$: total $= 2bc$
- **2 triangles** with legs $a, c$: total $= ac$
- **2 triangles** with legs $b, d$: total $= bd$

$$\text{parallelogram} = (a+b)(c+d) - 2bc - ac - bd$$
$$= ac + ad + bc + bd - 2bc - ac - bd = ad - bc \checkmark$$

## Key Insight

Each pair of triangles can be **slid** to fill the complementary gap on the opposite side of the bounding box, making it visually clear they exactly account for the surplus area.

views.name: Face On
views.description: Direct overhead view — see the parallelogram in the XY plane

steps.title: The Parallelogram
steps.description: The matrix $M = \begin{pmatrix}a & b\\ c & d\end{pmatrix}$ maps the unit square to a parallelogram with vertices $(0,0)$, $(a,c)$, $(a+b,c+d)$, $(b,d)$. Its area equals $|\det(M)| = |ad - bc|$.

steps.title: The Bounding Box
steps.description: Draw the bounding rectangle of size $(a+b) \times (c+d)$. Its area is $(a+b)(c+d) = ac + ad + bc + bd$. Our goal: subtract what's outside the parallelogram.

steps.title: Subtracting Rectangles
steps.description: Two congruent $b \times c$ rectangles sit in opposite corners of the bounding box, completely outside the parallelogram. Subtracting them removes $2bc$ from the total.

info.content:
Subtract **2 rectangles** of $b \times c$:

$2bc = 2({b})({c}) = {(2*b*c)}$

Running total removed: ${(2*b*c)}$

steps.title: Subtracting Triangles (ac)
steps.description: Two right triangles with legs $a$ and $c$ occupy opposite corners. Drag slider $t$ (or hit ▶) to watch each triangle slide to its complementary position, proving it exactly fills the surplus.

info.content:
Subtract **2 ac-triangles** of $\frac{1}{2}ac$:

$\frac{1}{2}({a})({c}) = {(a*c/2)}$ each

Total $ac = {(a*c)}$

Running total removed: ${(2*b*c + a*c)}$

steps.title: Subtracting Triangles (bd)
steps.description: Two right triangles with legs $b$ and $d$ fill the remaining surplus. Watch them slide to their complementary positions. After all subtractions, only the parallelogram area $ad - bc$ remains.

info.content:
Subtract **2 bd-triangles** of $\frac{1}{2}bd$:

$\frac{1}{2}({b})({d}) = {(b*d/2)}$ each

Total $bd = {(b*d)}$

Running total removed: ${(2*b*c + a*c + b*d)}$

steps.title: The Result: $ad - bc$
steps.description: After removing the 2 bc-rectangles, 2 ac-triangles, and 2 bd-triangles from the bounding box, exactly the parallelogram remains. The formula confirms $\det(M) = ad - bc$.

scenes.title: 3D Rotation Matrices
scenes.description: Three fundamental rotations — about the $x$, $y$, and $z$ axes — are the building blocks for all 3D rotations.
scenes.markdown:
# 3D Rotation Matrices

Rotation about the coordinate axes by angle $\theta$:

$$R_x(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix}1&0&0\\0&\cos\theta&-\sin\theta\\0&\sin\theta&\cos\theta\end{pmatrix}$$

$$R_y(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix}\cos\theta&0&\sin\theta\\0&1&0\\-\sin\theta&0&\cos\theta\end{pmatrix}$$

$$R_z(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix}\cos\theta&-\sin\theta&0\\\sin\theta&\cos\theta&0\\0&0&1\end{pmatrix}$$

## Properties of All Rotation Matrices

- $\det(R) = 1$ — preserves orientation and volume
- $R^T R = I$ — columns and rows are orthonormal
- $R^{-1} = R^T$ — transpose is the inverse
- Eigenvalues are $1, e^{i\theta}, e^{-i\theta}$

## The Rotation Axis

For $R_x$: the $x$-axis is fixed (eigenvalue 1)
For $R_y$: the $y$-axis is fixed
For $R_z$: the $z$-axis is fixed

This is why the **rotation axis is an eigenvector** with eigenvalue 1 — the key insight connecting rotation and eigenvalues!

> **Euler's Theorem**: Any 3D rotation can be written as a rotation about a single axis by some angle.

steps.title: A 3D reference vector
steps.description: Start with the vector $\mathbf{v} = (1, 1, 1)/\sqrt{3}$ shown in gold. We will rotate it about each coordinate axis.

steps.title: Rotation about $z$-axis
steps.description: $R_z(\theta)$ rotates in the $xy$-plane. The $z$-component of $\mathbf{v}$ is preserved. The $z$-axis is the **fixed** direction.

steps.title: Interactive: choose axis and angle
steps.description: Use slider $\theta$ to rotate. The $x$-component of the rotated vector follows $R_z$: $(v_x\cos\theta - v_y\sin\theta, v_x\sin\theta + v_y\cos\theta, v_z)$.
